docs.title: introduction
docs.text:
hello! daamath is a math library that gives you a lot of functions.

theres no particular order in which to learn daamath. explore around using the top nav bar

docs.title: arithmetic
docs.text: daamath has a complete set of arithmetic operators: ++ −− + − × ÷ ^ log √

docs.title: introduction
docs.text:
"daa, repeated addition is multiplication. repeated multiplication is exponentiation. whats the next level? and what do we have to repeat to get addition?"

the hyperoperation tower tells us everything we need to know

1 + 1 + 1 + … D times is 0 + D B + B + B + … D times is B × D B × B × B × … D times is Bᴰ B ^ B ^ B ^ … D times is ᴰB ⋮

this is the basic idea of hyperoperations. repeating the previous operation D times gives you the next operator. this gives us the tower: succ → add → mul → pow → spow → …

each operator also has two inverses which solve for the left or the right operand. for example, in the equation R = B ^ D, the left inverse (root) solves for the left operand B = ᴰ√R while the right inverse solves for the right operand D = logB(R).

the left inverse and right inverse of addition are same (subtraction). the left inverse and right inverse of multiplication are same (divison). so we say they have only one inverse.

docs.title: API implementation
docs.text: daamath is implemented as a programming math library. this section covers the language-agnostic specification

docs.title: succ(degree):
docs.text: signature: 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ → ℤ exceptions: (none) returns: 1 + degree

docs.title: pred(result):
docs.text: signature: 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ → ℤ exceptions: if signature is 𝕎 → 𝕎 and result is 0, raise a DomainError returns: result − 1

docs.title: add(base, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ  
exceptions: (none)  
returns: base + degree

docs.title: sub(result, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ 
returns: result − degree

docs.title: mul(base, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ
returns: base · degree

docs.title: div(result, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ
returns: result ∕ degree

docs.title: pow(base, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ
returns: base ^ degree

docs.title: root(result, degree):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ
returns: result ^ (1 / degree)

docs.title: log(result, base):
docs.text:
signature: 𝕎, 𝕎 → 𝕎 | ℤ, ℤ → ℤ | ℝ, ℝ → ℝ | ℂ, ℂ → ℂ 
returns: log_base(result)

tetration, super-root, super-logarithm are highly debated topics, especially for non-integer numbers. there is no canonical agreed-upon definition for them yet so until this is resolved, daamath shall not maintain hyperoperations of n ≥ 4

docs.title: argument order
docs.text:
at any hyperoperation level n, there are three operators: Hₙ(base, degree) = result Hₙ⁻ᴸ(result, degree) = base Hₙ⁻ᴿ(result, base) = degree

this works well for sub and div but there is confusion for log and root. in usual math notation, we write logₙ(x) and ⁿ√x. julia follows the order of math notation and writes log(n, x). python and C follow the reverse order log(x, n). daamath follows the reverse order log(x, n) and root(x, n) because it is consistent with the order of arguments in sub and div, i.e. log(result, base) and root(result, degree)

docs.title: type homomorphism
docs.text:
hyperoperations will return the same output type as its input type. if you pass in an integer, it will give an integer result. this is in contrast to dynamic type promotion in most languages, which eagerly promote integers to fractional numbers.

for example, in C, pow(2, 3) gives 8.0. in python, 2 ** -2 gives 0.25. with daamath, dm.pow(2, 3) will give 8, and dm.pow(2, -2) will raise a DomainError because 0.25 cannot be represented as an integer.

the biggest consequence to this is that integer arithmetic stays as integer arithmetic. unsigned integer arithmetic stays as unsigned integer arithmetic.

daamath maintains four number types: ℕ₀, ℤ, ℝ, ℂ

docs.title: notes
docs.text:
successor and predecessor are not the same as increment and decrement. the former two give a new answer, while the latter two imply mutating a variable. daamath implements the former two

actually, addition and multiplication have two inverses too. but since the left and right inverses do the same thing, we think of it as having one canonical inverse. the real hyperoperation tower ought to look like this:

the left and right inverse of multiplication are both division, but this isnt always true. for example, matrices may have two different division operators. one would multiply by the inverse of the left operand, another would multiply by the inverse of the right operand. but since daamath works with simple numbers, we do not make this distinction.

i dont typically see addition having two inverses..

docs.title: rant
docs.text:
the motivation for this hyperoperation table was that i wanted to find a way to unify all the arithmetic operators i knew under one structure. i found the hyperoperation tower a year before i made daamath, and when i applied it, it was surprisingly reliable. it helped me order them, figure out the relationship between pow-log-root, and it also helped me figure out the argument order for log and root. turns out that i should just follow the order that sub and div follow, which is to always put result as first argument.

i should really try to clarify the meaning of B D R as soon as possible

docs.title: interval
docs.text: intervals... defined on a dense set of numbers. daamath provides a few important functions for interval

docs.title: lt(a, b):
docs.text:
less than
a < b

docs.title: le(a, b):
docs.text:
less than or equal to
a ≤ b

docs.title: eq(a, b):
docs.text:
equal
a = b

docs.title: ne(a, b):
docs.text:
not equal
a ≠ b

docs.title: ge(a, b):
docs.text:
greater than or equal to
a > b

docs.title: gt(a, b):
docs.text:
greater than
a ≥ b

docs.title: oo(x, a, b):
docs.text:
in open interval
x ∈ (a, b)

docs.title: oc(x, a, b):
docs.text:
in left-open interval
x ∈ (a, b]

docs.title: co(x, a, b):
docs.text:
in right-open interval
x ∈ [a, b)

docs.title: cc(x, a, b):
docs.text:
in closed interval
x ∈ [a, b]

docs.title: logic
docs.text: we deal with logic that takes on False or True, F or T, 0 or 1, (uptack and downtack symbols here, daa!), OFF or ON, etc. we can create functions that take in these logical values and return some logical value.

docs.title: introduction
docs.text:
there are many many logical gates. we can construct all the possible gates in a very neat way:

with n inputs, we have 2^n possible permutations. for 2^n permutations, we have 2^2^n gates.

with n = 0, we have 2 nullary gates with n = 1, we have 4 unary gates with n = 2, we have 16 binary gates with n = 3, we suddenly have 256 ternary gates. since there are so many, and because they can be composed from binary gates anyway, daamath does not maintain gates of arity > 2.

docs.title: nullary gates
docs.text:
F false T true

there are technically boolean gates with zero inputs but they are better modelled as constants, in daamath.constants.

docs.title: unary gates
docs.text:
0 1 F F false (nullary) F T id T F not T T true (nullary)

the id gate is useless because writing id(value) is the same as writing value directly

thus we have one useful unary gate

docs.title: binary gates
docs.text:
A B 0 0 Neither 0 1 Second 1 0 First 1 1 Both

NSFB 0000 false (nullary) 0001 all 0010 ncon 0011 fst (unary) 0100 nimp 0101 snd (unary) 0110 xor 0111 or 1000 nor 1001 nxor 1010 nsnd (unary) 1011 con 1100 nfst (unary) 1101 imp 1110 nand 1111 (nullary)

fst, snd, nfst, nsnd are useless because writing fst(first, second) snd(first, second) nfst(first, second) nsnd(first, second) are the same as writing first second not(first) not(second) directly

thus we have 10 useful binary gates

docs.title: not(value):
docs.text: negation is [involutive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Involution_%28mathematics%29?wprov=sfla1). if you apply it twice, it gives you the original value.

docs.title: and(first, second):
docs.text: being associative, conjunction has a variadic version `all`. can also be thought of as intersection of sets

docs.title: or(first, second):
docs.text: being associative, disjunction has a variadic version `any`. can also be thought of as union of sets.

docs.title: xor(first, second):
docs.text: being associative, exclusive disjunction has a variadic version `parity_odd`. can also be thought of as symmonric difference of sets

docs.title: imp(first, second):
docs.text: material implication is distinctly directional. can also be thought of as difference of sets

docs.title: con(first, second):
docs.text: same as `imp(second, first)`. can also be thought of as difference of sets

docs.title: nand(first, second):
docs.text: not(and(first, second))

docs.title: nor(first, second):
docs.text: not(or(first, second))

docs.title: nxor(first, second):
docs.text: not(xor(first, second)). being associative, it has a variadic version `parity_even`

docs.title: nimp(first, second):
docs.text: not(imp(first, second))

docs.title: ncon(first. second):
docs.text: not(con(first, second))

docs.title: type support
docs.text: in fact, these logical gates can be applied to more than just logical values. we can apply them to integers in 2's complement bitwise, and also to sets. for example, in python, daamath.and supports bool, int, and set as input. in C, dm_and supports bool and int, applying them logically or bitwise respectively.

docs.title: notes
docs.text: nxor is used instead of xnor to preserve consistency.

docs.title: quantize
docs.text: quantization is such a wide topic.. but for daamath, i have implemented the few most important.

docs.title: floor(number):
docs.text: round towards −∞

docs.title: ceil(number):
docs.text: round towards +∞

docs.title: trunc(number):
docs.text: round towards 0

docs.title: away(number):
docs.text: round away from 0

docs.title: even(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest even integer (if not already an integer)

docs.title: odd(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest odd integer (if not already an integer)

docs.title: roundfloor(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties towards −∞

docs.title: roundceil(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties towards +∞

docs.title: roundtrunc(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties towards 0

docs.title: roundaway(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties away from 0

docs.title: roundeven(number):
docs.text:
round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties towards the nearest even integer
this is the recommended rounding method specified by IEEE. pretty cool.

docs.title: roundodd(number):
docs.text: round towards the nearest integer, breaking ties towards the nearest odd integer

docs.title: type signature
docs.text: these functions take in a real number, and return an integer.

docs.title: notes
docs.text:
if you need more advanced quantization, the pyquantize project may be useful for you

python has a round function that i despise. it has an optional ndigits parameter that, instead of quantizing to any arbitrary scaled lattice, it scales to quantum sizes of 1, 0.1 ,0.01, ... and on top of that, it will return a float if you provide this ndigits parameter, knowing full well that floats do not round decimal digits cleanly.

docs.title: trigonometry
docs.text: daamath maintains the full lattice of trigonometric functions. we also expose their relationship directly from [hypercomplex] algebra, believe it or not

docs.title: introduction
docs.text:
the trigonometric functions are derived from the exponential series

x⁰/0! + x¹/1! + x²/2! + x³/3! + …

where x = ι such that ι² ∈ {−1, 0, +1} (see why) totally we have 2 ⋅ 3 ⋅ 3P2 = 36 functions. 2 from the normal trig function and its inverse 3 from the three geometries 3P2 from a triangle of three sides, from which we take two sides

unfortunately, parabolic trigonometry is trivial, because . thus daamath excludes parabolic trigonometry and maintains 24 instead.

lastly, you may notice that the word 'angle' is never mentioned. that is because, while angles are convenient in circular geometry, in hyperbolic functions, we dont take the angle of anything anymore. we take the semiarea of the hyperbola with the x = y line. in fact, in circular geometry, what we are really describing when we say 'the angle' is the semiarea subtended by the section. this idea of semiarea extends to parabolic geometry as well.

docs.title: sin(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse in circular geometry

docs.title: cos(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse in circular geometry

docs.title: tan(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of opposite to adjacent in circular geometry

docs.title: cot(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of adjacent to opposite in circular geometry

docs.title: sec(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of hypotenuse to adjacent in circular geometry

docs.title: csc(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of hypotenuse to opposite in circular geometry

docs.title: asin(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: acos(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: atan(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of opposite to adjacent, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: acot(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of adjacent to opposite, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: asec(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of hypotenuse to adjacent, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: acsc(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of hypotenuse to opposite, return the semiarea in circular geometry

docs.title: sinh(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: cosh(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: tanh(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of opposite to adjacent in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: coth(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of adjacent to opposite in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: sech(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of hypotenuse to adjacent in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: csch(semiarea):
docs.text: given the semiarea, return the ratio of hypotenuse to opposite in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: asinh(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: acosh(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: atanh(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of opposite to adjacent, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: acoth(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of adjacent to opposite, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: asech(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of hypotenuse to adjacent, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: acsch(ratio):
docs.text: given the ratio of hypotenuse to opposite, return the semiarea in hyperbolic geometry

docs.title: rant
docs.text: why do trigonometric functions have anything to do with hypercomplex numbers? because the hypercomplex numbers encode geometries, which are beautifully expressed through these trig functions we have. there are three 2-dimensional unital algebrae: complex numbers, split-complex numbers, dual numbers. they correspond to circular, hyperbolic, and parabolic geometry.

docs.title: Index
docs.text: oh hey! choose your language! haha this isnt complete yet!

docs.title: C
docs.text: the C implementation isnt available for install yet.

docs.title: python
docs.text:
daamath is hosted on PyPI. simply run this in your terminal:

python -m pip install daamath

you may have to set up a venv. once youre done, try this:

import daamath as dm

print(dm.sqrt(4))
# 2
